items.title: Match loss to target type
items.avoid: Using CE on continuous targets or MSE on one-hot class vectors.
items.prefer: MSE/MAE for regression; BCE for binary; CE for single-label multi-class.
items.why: Each loss assumes a target structure and output activation (linear/sigmoid/softmax).
items.example: Price prediction: Linear output + MSELoss. 10-way digit: linear logits + CrossEntropyLoss.

items.title: Prefer MAE when outliers dominate
items.avoid: Defaulting to MSE on datasets with mislabeled or extreme y values.
items.prefer: Try MAE or Huber loss when a few points have outsized residuals.
items.why: Squaring inflates the influence of outliers on the average loss and gradients.
items.example: Housing data with one mis-entered $50M price: MAE training often beats MSE.

items.title: Use logits with framework CE
items.avoid: Manual softmax then log in fragile loops without log-sum-exp.
items.prefer: Pass logits to CrossEntropyLoss or use log_softmax + nll_loss.
items.why: Numerical stability and correct gradients when q(y) is tiny.
items.example: PyTorch: F.cross_entropy(logits, target) — not -log(softmax(logits)[y]) by hand.

items.title: Separate training loss from reporting metrics
items.avoid: Reporting training MSE as the final model quality without a held-out metric.
items.prefer: Track validation loss during training; report RMSE/MAE or log loss on test per RMSE & MAE and evaluation topics.
items.why: Training loss optimizes fit; stakeholders need generalization estimates on unseen data.
items.example: Log train MSE for convergence; publish test RMSE and MAE on the validation split.
